--- SOURCE 1: The importance of digital archiving - Quadient ---
URL: https://www.quadient.com/en/learn/digital-archiving/importance-of-digital-archiving

SUMMARY:
By safeguarding digital assets from loss and degradation, digital archiving systems ensure future access and retrieval . Thus, a digital archive comprises preserved, organized, and accessible digital materials.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Preserving digital collections - Archives sector ---
URL: https://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/

SUMMARY:
## Digital preservation workflows We have commissioned the Digital Preservation Coalition (DPC) to create Novice to Know-How, an online training resource as part of our Plugged In, Powered Up strategy. Active digital preservation can be defined in four stages: select and transfer, ingest, preserve and access. We have created a series of Plugged In, Powered Up advocacy resources to help explain the importance of developing digital preservation, access and engagement skills across the sector. As part of Our Digital Century, we commissioned the Digital Preservation Coalition (DPC) to develop a toolkit on creating and maintaining a digital asset register (DAR) for your archive. As part of our current digital capacity building strategy, Our Digital Century, The National Archives commissioned Kevin Bolton, Jan Whalen and Larysa Bolton to create a workflow for email preservation.

FULL CONTENT:
[Skip to main content](#breadcrumb-holder)



# Preserving digital collections

Digital preservation is a vital issue for almost all archives and there is an increasing demand for storage of, and access to, digital information in a discoverable format. There is an expectation that the vastly increased public access achieved in recent years will continue to expand.

## [Developing a digital preservation strategy and policy](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/developing-a-digital-preservation-strategy-and-policy/ "Developing a digital preservation strategy and policy")

The aim of your digital preservation strategy should be to achieve consistency in the management of digital records.

## [How to get started](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/how-to-get-started/ "How to get started")

Plan your first steps in digital preservation and discover the golden rules.

## [Digital preservation workflows](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-workflows/ "Digital preservation workflows")

As part of Plugged in Powered Up, we commissioned guidance on digital preservation workflows.

* [Introduction](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-workflows/introduction/ "Introduction")
* [1. Select and transfer](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-workflows/1-select-and-transfer/ "1. Select and transfer")
* [2. Ingest](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-workflows/2-ingest/ "2. Ingest")
* [3. Preserve](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-workflows/3-preserve/ "3. Preserve")
* [4. Access](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-workflows/4-access/ "4. Access")
* [Glossary](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-workflows/glossary/ "Glossary")

## [Novice to Know-How](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/novice-to-know-how/ "Novice to Know-How")

We have commissioned the Digital Preservation Coalition (DPC) to create Novice to Know-How, an online training resource as part of our Plugged In, Powered Up strategy.

## [Digital preservation architecture](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-architecture/ "Digital preservation architecture")

Active digital preservation can be defined in four stages: select and transfer, ingest, preserve and access.

* [University of Hull](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-architecture/university-of-hull/ "University of Hull")
* [South West Heritage Trust](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-architecture/south-west-heritage-trust/ "South West Heritage Trust")
* [University of Warwick: Modern Records Centre](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-architecture/university-of-warwick-modern-records-centre/ "University of Warwick: Modern Records Centre")

## [Advocacy resources](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/advocacy-resources/ "Advocacy resources")

We have created a series of Plugged In, Powered Up advocacy resources to help explain the importance of developing digital preservation, access and engagement skills across the sector.

## [Digital engagement toolkit](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-engagement-toolkit/ "Digital engagement toolkit")

Develop how you use your collections to tell stories online and engage audiences in different ways.

* [Toolkit introduction](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-engagement-toolkit/toolkit-introduction/ "Toolkit introduction")
* [Platform finder](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-engagement-toolkit/platform-finder/ "Platform finder")

* [Creative inspiration guide](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-engagement-toolkit/creative-inspiration-guide/ "Creative inspiration guide")

## [Digital Asset Registers](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-asset-registers/ "Digital Asset Registers")

As part of Our Digital Century, we commissioned the Digital Preservation Coalition (DPC) to develop a toolkit on creating and maintaining a digital asset register (DAR) for your archive.

## [Email preservation workflows](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/email-preservation-workflows/ "Email preservation workflows")

As part of our current digital capacity building strategy, Our Digital Century, The National Archives commissioned Kevin Bolton, Jan Whalen and Larysa Bolton to create a workflow for email preservation.

* [Introduction](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/email-preservation-workflows/introduction/ "Introduction")
* [1. Select and capture](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/email-preservation-workflows/1-select-and-capture/ "1. Select and capture")
* [2. Pre-ingest](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/email-preservation-workflows/2-pre-ingest/ "2. Pre-ingest")
* [3. Preserve](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/email-preservation-workflows/3-preserve/ "3. Preserve")
* [4. Access](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/email-preservation-workflows/4-access/ "4. Access")
* [Example workflows](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/email-preservation-workflows/example-workflows/ "Example workflows")

## [Line of business systems](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/line-of-business-systems/ "Line of business systems")

As part of our current digital capacity building strategy, Our Digital Century, The National Archives commissioned Kevin Bolton, Jan Whalen and Larysa Bolton to create a thought workflow for line of business systems.

* [Introduction](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/line-of-business-systems/introduction/ "Introduction")
* [1. Understanding stakeholders and the business](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/line-of-business-systems/1-understanding-stakeholders-and-the-business/ "1. Understanding stakeholders and the business")
* [2. Understanding the records](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/line-of-business-systems/2-understanding-the-records/ "2. Understanding the records")
* [3. Understanding retention and value](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/line-of-business-systems/3-understanding-retention-and-value/ "3. Understanding retention and value")
* [4. Analysing the system](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/line-of-business-systems/4-analysing-the-system/ "4. Analysing the system")
* [5. Identifying a preservation approach](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/line-of-business-systems/5-identifying-a-preservation-approach/ "5. Identifying a preservation approach")

## [Case studies](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/case-studies/ "Case studies")

These case studies offer practical examples of how archive services have implemented digital preservation processes and systems in their organisations.

* [University of Brighton Design Archives](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/case-studies/brighton-university-design-archives/ "University of Brighton Design Archives")
* [Gloucestershire Archives](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/case-studies/gloucestershire-archives/ "Gloucestershire Archives")
* [HSBC](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/case-studies/hsbc/ "HSBC")
* [The Postal Museum](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/case-studies/the-postal-museum/ "The Postal Museum")

## [Digital preservation tools and systems](/archives-sector/advice-and-guidance/managing-your-collection/preserving-digital-collections/digital-preservation-tools-systems/ "Digital preservation tools and systems")

Your approach to digital preservation should be modular and flexible, to ensure it is sustainable.

2.   [Manage Records](https://archives.un.org/content/manage-records)

Digital Preservation
--------------------

Digital Preservation is the active management and maintenance of digital objects (the files, or groups of files, that contain information in digital form) so they can be accessed and used by future users. It involves planning, resource allocation, and application of preservation methods and technologies, and it combines policies, strategies and actions to ensure access to reformatted and born-digital content, regardless of the challenges of media failure and technological change. The goal of digital preservation is the accurate rendering of authenticated content over time to ensure its authenticity, accessibility and usability.

The UN Archives is striving to ensure that all UN digital archives are preserved in compliance with international standards and are available to Member States, researchers, and the general public, meeting UN's accountability promise as an open international organization.

![Image 3](https://archives.un.org/sites/archives.un.org/files/styles/panopoly_image_full/public/cropped-challenges1.png?itok=yyGFqZyi)

![Image 4](https://archives.un.org/sites/archives.un.org/files/styles/panopoly_image_full/public/general/landing1.png?itok=YNGjxCIE)

What we are doing now
---------------------

The [digital preservation maturity model Opens a new window](https://ndsa.org/activities/levels-of-digital-preservation/) created by the [National Digital Stewardship Alliance (NDSA)Opens a new window](https://ndsa.org//) is used by UN Archives to benchmark and measure its level of preserving the digital collections under its physical custody. The NDSA model evaluates and categorizes an organization's digital preservation status into 4 levels based on 6 different elements:

*   Storage and Geographic Location
*   Data Integrity
*   Information Security
*   Metadata
*   File Formats
*   Physical Media

A set of open source tools have been tested and used in preserving digital collections;standard procedures have been established to ensure consistency in processing the digital collections;and digital preservation log sheets have been created to capture preservation metadata.

Benchmarking UN digital archives
--------------------------------

Currently, there are around 50 born digital archive collections in UN Archives custody. Measuring against the [NSDA model Opens a new window](https://ndsa.org/activities/levels-of-digital-preservation/), their digital preservation maturity level has improved over the past 4 years.

*   In 2014, we were at pre-level 1
*   In 2016, we were at level 1
*   In 2018, we are at level 2
*   In 2018, we are moving to level 3
*   In 2020 and beyond, we hope to get to level 4

SOP & Toolkit
-------------

Major steps in UN Archives digital preservation procedure includes:

*   Virus and spyware check
*   Making 1 master AIP(tool used: TeraCopy, HashDeep)
*   Making 2 validated identical copies of the master AIP (tool used:Disk Clone, HashDeep)
*   Accession the 3 AIP copies into 2 different geographical locations (tool used: HPE Content Manager)
*   Recording required digital preservation metadata(tool used: HPE Content Manager, Digital Collection Preservation Log)
*   Periodically validate the 3 AIP copies. (tool used: HashDeep, Digital Collection Preservation Log)

The SOP and toolkit will be published on this page soon. Please check back later.

--- SOURCE 4: Digital Preservation - Home | National Archives ---
URL: https://www.archives.gov/preservation/digital-preservation

SUMMARY:
Digital Preservation - Home | National Archives Two units at the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) have responsibility for maintaining the records in its holdings: _Preservation_, with the mandate to care for the physical holdings and perform preservation reformatting digitization of physical items, and _Digital Preservation_, with the mandate to support electronic records processing archivists, perform audits of the holdings, and assess the need to perform preservation actions. It applies to born-digital agency electronic records, digitized records from agencies, and NARA digitization for access and preservation reformatting. NARA is committed to collaboration with other national archives, libraries, and museums, and participates in international consortia such as the National Digital Stewardship Alliance, the Digital Preservation Coalition, and the Digital Library Federation.

![Image 1: National Archives Logo](https://www.archives.gov/sites/all/themes/nara/images/nara-print-logo.jpg)

Digital Preservation - Home
===========================

Two units at the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) have responsibility for maintaining the records in its holdings: _Preservation_, with the mandate to care for the physical holdings and perform preservation reformatting digitization of physical items, and _Digital Preservation_, with the mandate to support electronic records processing archivists, perform audits of the holdings, and assess the need to perform preservation actions.

The goal for digital preservation at NARA is to enable the continued, ongoing usability of these records, retaining the qualities of authenticity, accuracy, and functionality deemed to be essential and feasible. This is accomplished through risk assessment and planning, staff training, ongoing improvement of its processes and infrastructure, and internal and external collaboration.

NARA’s 2022-2026 [Digital Preservation Strategy](https://www.archives.gov/preservation/digital-preservation/strategy) guides its internal operations. It outlines the specific strategies that NARA will use in its digital preservation efforts and specifically addresses Infrastructure, Data Integrity, Format & Media Sustainability, Information Security, and Training. It applies to born-digital agency electronic records, digitized records from agencies, and NARA digitization for access and preservation reformatting.

Electronic records collections require ongoing risk assessment and preservation planning. NARA has developed, and continues to update, its [Digital Preservation Framework](https://www.archives.gov/preservation/digital-preservation/risk) for digital preservation risk assessment. The Framework is available for public comment, reuse, and adaptation.

NARA is committed to collaboration with other national archives, libraries, and museums, and participates in international consortia such as the [National Digital Stewardship Alliance](https://ndsa.org/), the [Digital Preservation Coalition](https://www.dpconline.org/), and the [Digital Library Federation](https://www.diglib.org/).

PLEASE NOTE: The Digital Preservation left-menu pages differ from other Preservation pages. The links are solely devoted to digital preservation topics.

This page was last reviewed on November 12, 2024.

# The importance of digital preservation to prevent data loss

![importance of digital preservation banner for blog post libnova blog website](https://libnova.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Blog-header-Light-17-1.png)

Is digital safeguarding not yet being implemented in your department?Do you wish to demonstrate and validate to your superiors **the importance of digital preservation** to prevent data loss and other related benefits?

Wondering **how to prevent data loss in a company**? Or perhaps you’ve recently learned about the digital safeguarding concept and want to delve into why **preserving your digital assets** is crucial?

In today’s post, we will detail the different reasons why safeguarding our data, collective knowledge, and cultural heritage is paramount for companies in different sectors, and for future generations.

If you want to anticipate future problems with your archives, the first step is to understand the significance of this topic, not only for companies but also for individuals.

Keep reading to discover why ensuring that historical records, [cultural artifacts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cultural_artifact), scientific research, and business information remain accessible and intact over the years is essential.

## **Understanding The Importance of Digital Preservation Through History**

While the concept of digital safeguarding goes back centuries, for some people, it seems to be a new concept that is just being discovered.

Early efforts in digital preservation focused on archiving digital content, such as scientific data, government records, and scholarly publications.

In the 1960s and 1970s, organizations like the [UK Data Archive](https://www.data-archive.ac.uk/) began storing digital data on magnetic tapes and other electronic media, laying the groundwork for future preservation practices.

During the 1980s and 1990s, as digital technologies became more prevalent, the importance of digital safeguarding became increasingly apparent.

The growth of the internet and the proliferation of digital content highlighted the need for systematic approaches to ensure the long-term accessibility of digital materials.

In the early 2000s, the community began developing standards, best practices, and tools to address the unique challenges of preserving digital information.

Initiatives such as the [Open Archival Information System (OAIS)](http://www.oais.info/) reference model provided frameworks for managing and preserving digital content over time.

As digital protection gained traction, governments, cultural institutions, and research organizations around the world started investing in digital conservation infrastructure and initiatives.

National libraries, archives, and museums established digital protection programs to safeguard their digital collections and ensure their accessibility for future generations.

In recent years, advancements in technologies, such as cloud storage, emulation, machine learning, and digital preservation platforms like [Libsafe Go](https://www.libnova.com/#products), have further expanded the capabilities of digital protection efforts.

## **Preserving information in the digital world**

As you can see, the history of preserving digital archives is marked by a continuous evolution in response to the changing landscape of digital technologies and the ongoing efforts to prevent data loss while protecting and preserving our digital heritage for future generations.

By safeguarding our digital assets, we protect our collective memory and knowledge for future generations. But what exactly does preserving digital information entail, and why is it so important?

Preserving digital archives involves the protection and maintenance of digital content, such as documents, images, videos, and other forms of digital media.

It ensures that this information remains accessible, usable, and authentic over time, despite technological advancements, hardware failures, data loss, or accidental deletions.

Collective memory, in the digital context, refers to the shared repository of information, experiences, and cultural heritage that society collectively retains and passes down through digital platforms and technologies.

This includes historical archives, cultural artifacts, scientific research, and various forms of knowledge stored in digital formats.

So, the significance of preserving digital collective memory cannot be overstated, because it serves as a vital link between the past, present, and future, allowing us to learn from history, celebrate achievements, and shape our future endeavors.

## **5 Reasons Why Digital Safeguarding is Essential for Data Integrity or to Prevent Data Loss**

### **Continued Accessibility**

Digital conservation ensures that valuable information remains accessible over time. By preserving digital materials, we prevent them from becoming obsolete or inaccessible due to changes in technology or software.

For example, digital archives maintained by institutions like the [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/) provide researchers with access to a wealth of historical documents and multimedia content, ensuring continuity in the exploration of human knowledge.

### **Safeguarding Authenticity**

Preserving digital objects involves maintaining their integrity and authenticity. When we safeguard the essential elements of digital assets, we ensure that their original meaning and purpose remain intact for future users.

### **Mitigating Threats**

Digital continuity faces threats such as loss of access means. Effective preservation strategies address these threats by working with standards, selecting relevant material, and placing it in secure locations.

### **Data Integrity**

By maintaining accuracy, consistency, and security across systems, we prevent negative business impacts and protect customer privacy.

### **Preventing Loss**

Digital protection prevents accidental loss of data. Whether due to cyber threats, hardware failures, or natural disasters, preserving digital assets is essential for long-term data reliability.

## **The Importance of Digital Preservation Across Various Sectors**

### **Libraries and Archives**

Our digital heritage spans centuries of human knowledge, from ancient manuscripts digitized for online access to contemporary business records stored in databases.

Without proper preservation, we risk losing valuable insights, stories, and innovations encoded in our digital artifacts.

For example, digital archives of ancient texts in museums or the digitization efforts of institutions like the [British Library](https://www.bl.uk/) allow access to historical manuscripts and rare documents that might otherwise be lost to time.

### **Healthcare**

By safeguarding medical records, research data, and patient information, we empower healthcare providers and policymakers to make informed decisions. Examples of essential digital assets include [Electronic Health Records (EHR)](https://www.healthit.gov/faq/what-electronic-health-record-ehr) systems, which centralize patient data, and digital archives of medical research papers, contributing to healthcare advancements.

### **Business and finances**

Safeguarding business and financial information includes protecting financial records, transaction logs, and regulatory documents.

Organizations maintain trust and transparency by ensuring compliance with legal and regulatory requirements. Additionally, facilitating audits, investigations, and financial analysis relies on accurate and accessible data.

Examples of crucial digital assets in this domain include digital backups of financial statements and cloud-based document management systems.

### **Academic and Research Institutions**

Guaranteeing data preservation in research, scholarly publications and academic journals fosters collaboration and knowledge sharing among researchers. They can also build on existing knowledge, resulting in discoveries and advances that benefit society.

It should be noted that rigorous scientific research is based on accurate and accessible data, so independent validation of results increases the credibility of scientific advances.

Digital assets worth mentioning in this area include institutional repositories and preservation initiatives led by academic libraries.

Such digital conservation efforts go beyond archiving, they safeguard our collective intellectual heritage for future generations, ensuring that knowledge endures.

### **Government and Public Sectors**

Preserving official records, government documents, and legislative archives ensures the continuity of governance. These records hold vital information about policies, decisions, and historical events.

It can also enable transparency by providing access to public information. Citizens have the right to know how their government operates, and preserving digital records ensures accountability and trust.

Beyond administrative functions, digital conservation safeguards our cultural and historical heritage as well. By preserving artifacts, photographs, and manuscripts, we pass down our collective memory to future generations.

[National Archives Digital Preservation Programs](https://www.archives.gov/preservation/digital-preservation) are an example of important initiatives to safeguard invaluable collections. These programs ensure that historical documents remain accessible and intact.

### **Media and Entertainment**

Safeguarding digital assets, ranging from audiovisual content to multimedia productions is essential because these assets represent creativity, investment, and cultural value.

Films, music, and artworks are cultural artifacts that shape our collective identity.

Whether it’s classic films or music archives, ensuring availability benefits researchers, students, and enthusiasts.

### **Legal and Law Enforcement**

Safeguarding legal documents, court records, and evidence materials ensures the continuity of justice.

These records hold critical information about legal proceedings, case details, and historical legal events, maintaining the accuracy and reliability of evidence, which is crucial for fair legal processes.

For example, digital archives of court documents,centralize court records, making them accessible for legal professionals, researchers, and the public.

## **More Benefits of Digital Conservation**

### **Mitigating Technological Obsolescence**

Technology evolves rapidly, rendering older file formats and software obsolete. Digital safeguarding ensures that content remains accessible despite these changes by migrating data to newer formats and platforms.

So, by preserving data in formats compatible with modern technology, we ensure its accessibility for future generations.

For instance, initiatives like the [Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine](https://help.archive.org/help/wayback-machine-general-information/) allow users to access historical web pages, bridging the gap between past and present online content.

### **Guarding Against Hardware Failures**

Hardware failures are inevitable, from hard drive crashes to server malfunctions. Digital conservation involves redundancy—storing copies of data across multiple locations and formats—to minimize the impact of hardware failures.

When we maintain redundant copies of data, we safeguard against the risk of data loss due to hardware failures.

For example, cloud storage providers like [Amazon Web Services](https://aws.amazon.com/) offer redundancy across multiple data centers, ensuring data durability and availability.

***“Data loss is not just an inconvenience, it’s a piece of our shared history that slips away – that’s one of the main reasons why digital preservation is important”.***

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital preservation refers to the active management and maintenance of digital materials, ensuring their accessibility and usability over time to prevent data loss and obsolescence.

Effective digital preservation involves workflows: selecting, ingesting, preserving, and providing access to digital objects, ensuring they remain accessible despite technological changes and media failures.

Metadata standards play a crucial role in digital preservation by providing essential information about digital objects, ensuring their discoverability, authenticity, and facilitating consistent management across various platforms.
